the mcp server -- how does it work? when the mcp server runs `who` tool what do we show it if it's been started without the context of an agent?

with no agent context it should create an agent context for that session, ideally showing it as "chatgpt/echo123" for chatgpt or "grok/echo123" for grok -- I think we can associate, at least for chatgpt, based on session-id x-openai header or something like that

Good signal: the MCP `initialize` handshake already receives `clientInfo` (name/version) from the connecting client and even logs it (`src/cli/mcp/access_log.rs:31-32`) тАФ but it's discarded, not threaded through to session resolution. That's a more reliable identity source than sniffing HTTP headers, since header-sniffing (`x-openai-...`) only works in `--http` mode; the default stdio transport (which is how most harnesses spawn this) has no headers at all, only inherited env vars.

My recommendation: capture `clientInfo.name` at `initialize` time, stash it against the connection/session in the daemon, and have `rpc_who`'s no-anchor fallback auto-provision a session (`<clientInfo.name>/<random-suffix>`, matching the existing `agent/session-fragment` convention already visible in the fabric тАФ e.g. `@codex/te-18c0e7f875d4bb90-3`) instead of collapsing to an anonymous empty-string snapshot.

Main tradeoff: this turns `who` from a pure read into something with a side effect (creates a session record on first call), and `clientInfo.name` is self-reported by the client so it's identity-by-assertion, not verified тАФ fine for display/awareness purposes, not for anything security-sensitive. Also need to decide persistence: keyed per MCP connection (dies with the process) vs. something stabler if the same external session reconnects.

Want me to look at how sessions get created/registered elsewhere in the daemon (to see if there's already a "create session" path `rpc_who` could call into), before sketching an actual plan?
